coredata.dc:description: Green carbon materials (GCMs) have attracted great attention as they have unique mechanical, optical, and electrical properties that make them suitable for the development of the next generation of miniature, low-power, and mobile sensors. In this review paper, an attempt has been made to explore green carbon materials where their synthesis methods, conjugation with other types of nanomaterials to form nanocomposites, thus combining different properties into one single novel material, and environmental analysis applications are detailed. Green carbon materials have great significance in the analysis of environmental pollutants because they increase the effective area of an electrode, improve the rate of electron transfer between the analytes and the electrode, and help in catalysis, which in turn increases the efficacy of an electrochemical reaction, absorption, detection, and elimination. Moreover, functionalized green carbon materials when used in sensors for fast, economic environmental pollutant analysis are found to be highly sensitive to monitoring heavy metals and other polluting impurities in air, water sources, soils, etc. This review covers research and advancements in a broad span from 2013 to 2021, and some of the leading and fascinating research articles from the early years of the century have also been incorporated.

head.ce:title: Green Carbon Materials for the Analysis of Environmental Pollutants

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green carbon materials (GCMs) have attracted great attention as they have unique mechanical, optical, and electrical properties that make them suitable for the development of the next generation of miniature, low-power, and mobile sensors. In this review paper, an attempt has been made to explore green carbon materials where their synthesis methods, conjugation with other types of nanomaterials to form nanocomposites, thus combining different properties into one single novel material, and environmental analysis applications are detailed. Green carbon materials have great significance in the analysis of environmental pollutants because they increase the effective area of an electrode, improve the rate of electron transfer between the analytes and the electrode, and help in catalysis, which in turn increases the efficacy of an electrochemical reaction, absorption, detection, and elimination. Moreover, functionalized green carbon materials when used in sensors for fast, economic environmental pollutant analysis are found to be highly sensitive to monitoring heavy metals and other polluting impurities in air, water sources, soils, etc. This review covers research and advancements in a broad span from 2013 to 2021, and some of the leading and fascinating research articles from the early years of the century have also been incorporated.

ce:section.ce:para: Solutions that are effective as well as highly sustainable are needed to face and fulfil the challenges put forth by the different energy and other related demands of a densely populated world and the associated consequences of these demands on the environment. Pollutants are defined as chemical or biological items that degrade or impair the integrity and purity of the environment's various characteristics, such as freshwater, land-vegetation, or air [1]. The rapid speed of urbanisation and industrialization has contributed to the environmental disaster. The most difficult task in eco-chemistry is to discover, quantify, and characterise toxicants so that treatment remedies can be considered. Because the proportion of such contaminants, such as heavy metals, is sometimes low in the external environment, it is difficult to detect them using traditional physical methods [2]. However, even small levels of these contaminants can have a significant impact on the environment, such as bioaccumulation [3].

The World Health Organization (WHO) has established safe exposure levels for these contaminants, which include several toxic metal ions. These contaminants engage with hormone receptors in humans, causing detrimental effects on hormonal secretions and thus interfering with human, wildlife, and marine organisms' health [3] To maintain a healthy environment, portable sensors need to be developed that can monitor the presence of toxic pollutants and help with ecological maintenance [1] This is not an insignificant task, as the novel materials used for developing such sensors should be upgradable, industrially and economically feasible, and fabricated using a renewable green resource. They should, of course, show efficient performance in renewable energy conversion as well as their applications in environmental chemistry and should be no less efficient than existing technologies. For sensing and detecting purposes, green carbon materials-based sensing devices and technologies have emerged significantly in the past few years. [Fig. 1 ]

Reduction or elimination of these pollutants can be done only when accurate detection procedures have been carried out to determine which type of pollutant is present in the environment so that specific treatment procedures can be employed [1] In the last few years, many methodologies have evolved to analyse environmental pollutants from various sources. Some of these technologies are liquid chromatography and fluorescence detection. However, there are several disadvantages associated with these methods. These include increased operation time, complex instrumentation inconvenience, high installation and operation costs, and stringent detection conditions.

Novel sensing methodologies based on green and sustainable carbon-based technologies have been studied to overcome these restrictions and to carry out the detection of environmental toxins. These methods rely on electrochemical and chemical analyses through the use of sensors. Many methodologies, including coulometry, conductometry, and potential analysis, are used in these efforts. The advantages of these technologies are as follows: [4,5]
•
Quick response time
•
Miniaturization of the instrumentation
•
High selectiveness and sensitivity towards environmental pollutants
•
Economical
•
Simplified operation

Chemical sensors used earlier to detect analytes in the environment had several limitations. The dearth of selectivity was one of the most common flaws. This rendered the product unproductive because it was not able to work efficiently in real-world conditions such as temperature fluctuations, varying humidity, and different species. The introduction of green carbon-based materials as chemical or electrochemical sensors not only contributes towards a sustainable future but also exhibits increased selectivity and other advantages useful for detecting desired analytes, thus posing a pioneering power to the green carbon-based materials to function as eco-pollutant sensors[6,7]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green carbon-based materials and their synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Recent endeavours have been made to develop adaptable and cost-effective strategies for addressing environmental concerns using both sustainable and multifunctional materials [8]. Out of so many new materials discovered, carbon-based materials synthesised using green chemistry are one of the primary choices for researchers as they are desirable due to good structural stability, enormous catalytic activity and selective interaction has led to the advancement of the fields like O2 evolving and reducing reactions, Li-ion /Na-ion batteries and more in energy hoarding [9] These materials include carbon nanotubes, 0D-C60 fullerenes, mesoporous carbon, graphene sheets, carbon dots, fullerenes, etc. [3]

Green chemistry utilises green/natural sources like plants and micro-organisms or their by-products for the synthesis of desired materials like pseudocapacitive electrode materials for energy storage designed with CNT, graphene, 3D graphene and their hybrids functionalized with like MnO2, CuO Mn3O4, Co9S8, NiCo2O4, PANI and more[10] Metals or metal oxides can be found in almost all natural sources, including plants and microbes. These metals or their oxides are usually found in nanometre-sized particles. As a result, they can be used as catalysts in the production of carbon-based products. Green chemistry regulations ensure that the synthesis or use of poisonous or carcinogenic compounds is reduced or eliminated during the procedures. Materials synthesised from this approach are sustainable and decrease health risks associated with conventionally synthesised materials. Green carbon-based materials have been demonstrated to be a suitable and strong electrochemical platform for their application in environmental analytical chemistry. Further, functionalization of their surfaces using various organic, inorganic, or biomaterial groups increases their robustness and performance. [11]

Preparation of green carbon materials can involve various templates, and by changing the template structure, variations in the structure of the carbon material can be seen. Metals or metal oxides might not appeal as renewable sources, but their abundance in the earth’s crust makes them suitable to be used as pre-cursors in carbon material synthesis. Ethylene or natural gas reserves can act as a potential carbon source.

An overview of statistics of the increased number of publications in the field of Au-nanoparticles-based optical sensors and their composites with green carbon materials as per Scopus is summarised in the table below (Table 1 ).

Carbon-based materials can be produced by various techniques, such as chemical, physical, biological, or hybrid. However, manufacturing facilitates the utility of eco-friendly sources such as plants, microbes (e.g., bacteria and fungi), algae, enzymes, and industrial or agricultural waste products by using biological procedures [13]. Green chemistry fundamentals also aid in the monitoring of heavy metal contaminants in the environment and have emerged as a potential option in recent years owing to their merits over existing pollutant detection methods. The benefits of this method include numerous supplies, biocompatibility, biodegradability, non-hazardousness, fast synthesizing, and feasible budget. [14] Combining the properties of carbon materials and those of biomolecules produces a synergistic effect that helps develop ideal sensors for analysing contaminants in the environment. [Fig. 4 ]

Green solvents (i.e., supercritical fluids and amphiphilic solvents), biopolymers, and biomolecules are some naturally derived reducing agents that help to synthesise green materials [12] Plants, microbes, or various biologically produced molecules serve as the sources of such materials. In the case of micro-organisms, these materials form in the extracellular region after the enzymes of the microbes reduce the substrates[15] In plants, naturally produced capping or reducing agents help to synthesise stable carbon materials. Secondary metabolites of plants like alkaloids, carbohydrates, proteins, and phenols are used for environmental detection. The structure of the synthesised carbon-based material depends on the extent to which a plant extract can stabilise the particles formed [1]

Recently, green synthesised carbon-based materials oriented towards a versatile sensing approach have been explored by various scientists. [Fig. 5 ]. Ashrafi Tafreshi and the team synthesised carbon dots via the green route, using the pesticide-free-cauliflower plant extract by the carbonization process. [16]. Similarly, Raji Atchudan et al. used the hydrothermal method for the preparation of HN-CDs using the suspension of well-grounded dwarf banana peel. [17] Fanny J. González-Fuentes and his team used the aqueous extract of algae, Sargassum sp. (Ssp), containing two species: natans and fluitans, which was used as a reducing and stabilising agent, and the precursor solution (HAuCl4) was used for the synthesis of AuNPs at room temperature, 25 °C. [18] Pooja Chauhan et al. used the incineration process on agarose waste to get carbon-dots. [19]. A.B. Hernández-Abreu and his team’s research yielded a Kraft Lignin Precipitate (KLP)-based activated carbon adsorbent synthesis consisting of washing, impregnating, drying, and carbonization steps. [20] Mohamed E. Mahmoud and the team suggested a simple and eco-friendly method where 700 W microwave-assisted heating of a solution of 6 g starch in 40 mL of deionized water for 10 minutes produced CQDs. Then, using acetic acid as a catalyst, a polymeric material based on poly(anthranilic acid-formaldehyde-phthalic acid/PAFP) was synthesised by condensation polymerization of 0.1 mol of anthranilic acid and phthalic acid with 0.2 mol of formaldehyde. This low-cost, simple adsorption technique removed 95.5–98.0% of U(VI) ions at 30 - 90 mg/L[21] Raja Dinesh kumar and Sundaramurthy Devikala's research team[22] developed a synthesis method from betel leaves. The process consisted of washing, juice extraction, heating, cooling, filtration, and centrifugation steps to obtain CQDs that detect Fe3+ in an aqueous medium in the presence of other metal ions like (Mg2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Fe2+) at the same concentration. A cotton-based green synthesised carbon dot development procedure was developed by Xiangping Wen and her team. The process consisted of cotton pyrolysis, mechanical grinding, water dispersion, centrifugation, dialysis, and microwave treatment. The fabricated CDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility, high fluorescent quantum yield, low toxicity, and satisfactory chemical stability. The as-prepared CDs can selectively and sensitively detect Fe3+ ions in a linear range of [5 × 10-8] to [1 × 10-5M], with a detection limit as low as 2.7 × 10-8M. [23]

Zero-dimensional nanomaterial GQDs are small fragments of graphene (lateral dimensions less than 100 nm) and consist of a monolayer or a few layers of graphene. Citric acid and glucose are popular carbon precursors for the preparation of GQDs because of their natural origins, low cost, easy availability, and biocompatibility. Moreover, the GQDs so obtained show photoluminescence.

Oxidation methods, laser ablation, controllable synthesis, pyrolysis, hydrothermal methods, and microwave irradiation method are used for the synthesis of GQDs. These methods are Low-cost, easy, and eco-friendly as they depend on green raw materials and biomass waste. The salient features of these methods are summarised in (Table 2).

Due to biocompatibility, low toxicity, high surface area, abundance of functional groups, easy functionalization with inorganic, organic, or biological molecules, chemically stable, water-soluble, inert, robust, and photo-stable, attractive optical absorption properties, excellent photoluminescence property (PL), and high-speed electron transport, GQDs can act as good sensing materials, and peroxidase mimetic activity is useful for the development of electrocatalytic H2O2 detectors.

GQDs can be successfully employed in biosensors, fuel cells, electrochemical immunosensors for biomedical applications, supercapacitors, and photovoltaic cells. They are also used as fluorescent tags on molecular targets in bioimaging.[34]

Synthetic methods for green carbon materials (GCMs) are classified into three types: top-down approaches, bottom-up strategies, and green methods. Top-down strategies need suitable sources. Carbon nanotubes, graphite are sources for carbon dots synthesis. Chemical oxidation, electrochemical synthesis, laser passivation, and arc discharge methods are some of the approaches used for top-down methods. Longer reaction times, harsh reaction conditions, costly and special equipment, and expensive raw materials are drawbacks of such top-down methods. Bottom-up methods require right molecular precursors for the preparation of GCMs, including carbon dots. Examples of this strategy include hydrothermal, microwave irradiation, ultrasonic irradiation, solution chemistry approaches, etc. Hazardous reagents and large volumes of chemical solvents are needed in the solvothermal method. Cost-effectiveness, simplicity, novelty, non-toxicity, organic solvent-free are some of the needed advantages of green methods for the fabrication of GCMs. [35], [36]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green carbon materials vs. non-green carbon materials
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the major differences between carbon materials manufactured using principles of green chemistry and materials manufactured using other synthetic techniques (Table 3 ):
The differences listed above are some of the many advantages that green carbon materials have over synthetic materials. [37]

Green carbon materials have green and non-green applications with very low limits of detection. The experimental setup, conditions, electrode system employed, the efficiency of detection, advantages, disadvantages, and limitations of the green carbon materials to a selected application field are summarised in the (Table 4) below.
A1: Paracetamol oxidation is facilitated.
A2: Simultaneous determination of PCT with two or more compounds.
A3: Simple electrode fabrication
A4: The sensor has a long shelf life.
A5: Low LOD.
A6: Affordability
A7: Selective and sensitive
A8: Quick analysis
A9: Biocompatibility
A10: Aims to detect total chromium in the sample.
A11: No pre-reduction or oxidation needed.
A12: Excessive quantum yield
D1: High LOD.
D2: Expensive electrode materials.
D3: Limited or unknown shelf-life stability of the sensor.
D4: Unknown or insufficient interference tests.
D5: Sensor reproducibility is limited.
D6: Fabrication of complex electrodes
D7: Constant optimum pH maintenance is needed.
D8: BPA has a certain degree of inevitable signal noise.
D9: Less effective than electrochemical technique
D10: Concentration of QD-based substrate > 100 nM. It gives a lot of noise.
D11: Requires a high and constantly monitored temperature (200 °C).

Carbon-based materials have distinct features that make them suitable for developing sensors. However, there are certain limitations associated with these features. For example, carbon nanomaterials synthesised using the principles of green chemistry have large surface areas. This increases their ability to undergo van der Waals interactions, resulting in their agglomeration. Also, carbon materials are chemically inert, thus affecting their solubility, degradability, and performance. Functionalized carbon materials, on the other hand, have a lower tendency to agglomerate, increased solubility, and flexible electronic properties that are useful for fabricating sensors [1] The functional entities such as macromolecules or biomolecules anchored on the surface of carbon nanomaterials help them cooperate, identify, and react with the target analytes to carry out an effective detection process.
Carbon materials can be functionalized using two main methods: covalent and non-covalent modifications.

The adhesion of functional groups to the carbon-based material matrix is persistent and resilient thanks to covalent functionalization. This enables them to be used in a wide range of situations, including those with adverse environments. The process of covalent functionalization entails a chemical reaction between the functional moiety and the carbon nanomaterial's terminal or conjugated interface[43]

In the year 2009, Zhang and his team developed a systematic technique for modifying the surface of carbon-based materials by various functional groups [3]. They involved the development of an in-situ zwitterion intermediate, formed due to the distribution of 4-Dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP) and acetylene dicarboxylates on the surface. A range of functional channels are formed on the walls of carbon-based materials. Many reactions can be carried out after functionalization, like olefin cross-metathesis reaction, thiol-ene addition, and 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition. This helps to introduce important groups such as propargyl, carboxylic, alkyl triazole, allyl, thioalkyl chain, crown ether, hexafluoroisopropanol (HFIP) assemblies, and arene onto the carbon surface. These engineered groups are capable of selective recognition of volatile environmental pollutants [44]

Schnorr’s research group conducted experiments with sensors fabricated with functionalized green carbon-based materials [45]. They concluded that these sensors were chemiresistive and could detect the presence of environmental pollutants such as cyclohexane, nitromethane, and vapour signatures in parts per million (ppm). These sensors were also reported to be reproducible and stable during detection procedures. They also work efficiently in humid conditions. Pendant hexafluoroisopropanol (HFIP) was used to detect DMMP (dimethyl methyl phosphonate) The degree of functionalization when calculated was seventy-five carbons for one HFIP. To do this functionalization, in-situ formation of the aryl diazonium ion at a temperature of 750 °C was carried out. Many metal oxides can be attached covalently to carbon-based materials using the sol-gel method. These fabricated sensors detect a wide range of pollutants like acetylene gas, NO2, and NO at 300 °C [46]. Many such functionalization methods can be carried out for creating electrochemical sensors.

Non-covalent complexation of carbon materials happens when tiny molecules and surfactants are deposited on the carbon surface via physical absorption. Hydrophobic or "π-π" interactions exist between these compounds and the carbon surface. Dais's research group is credited with linking protein molecules on CNT surfaces. [47] A bifunctional entity was used for this process, which consisted of pyrene and succinimidyl esters as adsorbents [48] The amino acids of proteins underwent nucleophilic substitution reactions, which were responsible for setting up these interactions. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopic (AFM) analysis were carried out to confirm these attachments. Two proteins, streptavidin and ferritin, were attached with biotinyl-3,6-dioxaoctanediamine. Other functional groups that can be attached using this process are cyclodextrin, amino acids, and boronic acid. These functionalities, attached covalently to the carbon surfaces, can detect organic molecules such as TNT (Trinitrotoluene) and glucose, respectively [49]. Gas sensors are reported to have increased selectivity by using mixtures of macromolecules or small molecules [7]. Cobalt oxide (Co3O4) nanospindles are decorated on a reduced graphene oxide (RGO) surface when thermal decomposition of a cobalt benzoate dihydrazinate precursor was done. The RGO/Co3O4 composite electrode exhibits high nitrite sensitivity due to the excellent electrocatalytic activity of Co3O4 and the large specific surface area of the RGO. The electrode was highly selective even in the presence of various common ions (Fig. 6 ) [100].

The ideology of green chemistry is fresh and gives a schematic solution to the environmental hazard caused by the conventional manufacturing principles of materials. Discussed below are the advantages of green carbon-based materials for humans as well as other aspects [50]

ce:list-item.ce:para: Reduced toxic chemical emissions into the air during the manufacturing process, resulting in lung safety and cleaner air
Reduced pollutant emissions in water bodies result in an abundance of clean drinking and recreational water.
Safety of labourers and workers involved in the manufacturing process
Eco-friendly products that are safe to use

ce:section.ce:para:
Benefits to the environment: [51]
•
Green chemicals used during the development of green carbon-based materials are reusable and can even be recovered for future usage, hence conserving the environment.
•
There is no harmful effect on plants and animals as the resources used and the manufacturing process are eco-friendly.
•
A decreased likelihood of global warming
•
Reduced use of landfills and the release of biodegradable waste

The use of biodegradable and renewable sources and wastes as precursors for designing carbon-based materials is important for the sustainability of the environment. Creative methods have been reported in the past few years for synthesising green carbon materials that provide specialised advantages compared to other traditional materials [45] Due to the unique chemical composition of the substrates used in the fabrication of green carbon materials such as polysaccharides [Fig. 7 ], cellulosic components, algae, lignin, etc. The surface chemistry of such green carbons is much more complex compared to the other carbon materials synthesised by non-greener technologies. This complexity gives unique features to these carbon materials and has become a key aspect guiding their function in many areas of adsorption, catalysis, etc.

Analytical and bioanalytical chemistry used in environmental monitoring is focusing on greener aspects of synthesising carbon materials as well as miniaturising them for easy use and portability. The word "miniaturization" refers to the manufacturing of systems with smaller sizes when compared to conventional devices used [9] The fundamental objectives of using green chemistry include reducing the use of toxic chemicals in the analytical process, introducing environment-friendly techniques, minimising the energy requirement, producing economical systems, and easy portability [2]. Several matrices can be employed to assess the green aspects of various sensors, techniques, etc. developed, of which the National Environmental Methods Index (NEMI) is one of the most widely used approaches [52]

Chemical or electrochemical sensors made up of green carbon-based materials are employed for environmental detection and identification of pollutants due to [12]: increased selectivity and sensitivity; small size; economical, portable, eco-friendly fabrication methods; and simultaneous pollutant detection.

Traditional methods for environmental monitoring involve expensive equipment, are mostly time-consuming, require skilled manpower, and cannot detect low sample concentrations. Biosensors with carbon bases [53] offer higher efficacy and accuracy, overcome the limitations of and can complement laboratory-based techniques, and can also be applied for remote testing. The biosensor’s components [Fig. 8 ] include a biorecognition element/bioreceptor to interact with a specific analyte; the transducer assesses this interaction and produces a signal proportional to the concentration of the analyte. The generated signal is then amplified and processed by the electronics system, enabling low concentration detection. Finally, the signal processor displays the user-friendly quantifiable signal. [54]

In the case of the electrochemical biosensor [Fig. 9 ], the redox chemical groups enable the transformation of molecular interactions such as pollutant capture or DNA hybridization into electrical signals that correspond to a target analyte(s). Electro-chemical nucleic acid (NA) biosensors have portability, sensitivity, specificity, cost-effectiveness, small molecule detection, competence to be employed in turbid fluids, and ease of application. In collective, it integrates the inherent bio-selectivity of NA with the sensitivity of electroanalytical methods. The NA component in the biosensor detects the analyte(s) due to binding or catalytic events.

The affinity-based NA biosensor is known as Aptasensor. The aptamer (ssRNA or ssDNA) can selectively bind to specific target elements, like carbohydrates, proteins, peptides, toxins, small molecules, and cells.

Abu Hashem and his team developed a simplified example of an affinity mode electrochemical NA biosensor for Salmonella detection, where the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was upgraded with graphene oxide (GO) and gold (Au) nanoparticles (GNPs) to improve electron transfer capabilities. [56]
Different types of nanomaterials, synthesised using green chemistry, can be used for fabricating these carbon-based sensors (Table 5 ) [57]

Hydrothermally synthesised nano-hybrid (SnO2) quantum dots-C60 can be applied as a gas sensor. Various gases, including 700 ppm H2S, can be studied at temperatures ranging from 100 °C to 200 °C. The results showcased extensive selectivity and response behaviour of the sensing system for all considered gases, where H2S was the gas with the highest response, due to an increased ratio between the sensor’s resistance in air and the tested gaseous atmosphere. [60]

In ethanol gas sensing, fullerenes, or fullerene-decorated zinc oxide (ZnO) tetrapod materials, are used.C60 clusters are put onto the surface of ZnO to alter the absorption and luminescence characteristics of the tetrapod of ZnO. [61]
C20 fullerenes are also exploited for gas (mainly for diatomic and rare gaseous materials) sensing applications. C20 is remarkable for the detection of gases like CO, NO, and oxygen.

The adsorption of rare gas molecules by C20 significantly changed its electronic nature, especially for Sc-doped C20. Then the adsorption of rare gases on the Sc-doped C20 surface is instrumental for advanced gas adsorbent and sensor developments. [62]

For the quality of the water for various applications and to even confirm its potability, fullerene-based nanoscale composites are employed where they can detect inorganic and organic contaminants. [63]

To detect the presence of these gases in the environment, chemiresistive sensors fabricated using carbon nanotubes were first developed by Rigoni and coworkers [67] These sensors showed outstanding sensitivity to NO2 and NH3 at concentrations up to 20 ppm. Single-walled CNTs were employed to construct such sensors by the di-electrophoresis technique. Huh et al. developed a carbon nanotube-based chemical sensor that could detect ammonia environmental contamination at values up to 5 ppm around the same time. Several studies have inferred that the sensitivity of these sensors to ammonia or nitrogen dioxide can be increased by choosing a suitable catalyst. This conclusion was used by Wang and the team to synthesise such CNT-based sensors using nickel as a catalyst. The sensor could detect ammonia gas effortlessly at concentrations of up to 200 ppm [68] Pristine CNT-based sensors were presented by Li et al., which detected NO2 concentrations between 6 and 100 ppm [69]

Methane and hydrogen are considered explosives. Hence, even if they are present in small concentrations in the environment, they are hazardous. Additionally, methane is a greenhouse gas, contributing to global warming. For these reasons, detection of CH4 and H2 has become important to prevent explosions in centres or systems where they are produced. Production of methane also needs to be monitored to avoid cases of leakage, as that can be an economic as well as environmental loss. Composites of carbon materials and nanoparticles are generally used for developing sensors that are sensitive to methane and hydrogen. Experimental analysis and theoretical calculations have demonstrated that various carbon materials functionalized with platinum or palladium show higher sensitivity towards hydrogen gas. Using an electron beam, Kong et al. deposited palladium on the carbon surface. As a hydrogen detector, the resulting sensor worked well. Modulating the size of nanoparticles can improve a sensor's sensitivity and selectivity. This was further proved by Penner et al. who demonstrated that functionalized carbon materials demonstrated rapid sensing of hydrogen gas when the diameter of the nanoparticles was changed. Lu and the team demonstrated the detection of methane gas by using carbon-based sensors functionalized with palladium. Methane concentration in the range of 6–100 ppm was detected using these sensors [71] Subsequent exposure to UV radiation increased the detection parameters. In a different approach, Humayun et al. showed that carbon-based sensors, functionalized with nanoparticles of metal oxide (either ZnO or SnO2), efficiently detected CH4 by deposition of an atomic layer. The fabricated sensors were highly sensitive as well as selective towards CH4 [72]

It is a poisonous, toxic gas that is colourless, tasteless, and odourless. The main sources of CO emission are industries, incomplete combustion of exhaust, and fuels. OSHA has announced the permissible limit of CO in the environment between 35 ppm and 200 ppm, for about 8-h [73]. CO has a greater affinity to form complexes with haemoglobin (Hb), the blood-carrying metalloporphyrin present in the human body. The rate of formation of the CO-Hb complex is greater than the rate of formation of the O2-Hb complex [74]. The presence of CO in the human body is highly injurious to health. Sensors based on carbon materials are more effective in detecting CO as compared to other chemical sensors. The detection mechanism of these sensors is based on resonance frequency. Fluctuations in resonance frequency indicate that CO is present in the environment. The sensitivity of these carbon-based sensors can be increased by functionalizing them with aluminium, nitrogen, and boron. Sensors doped with carboxylic acid and sulfonate groups detected CO at about the concentration of 1 ppm. These functional groups were attached covalently to the sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensors for pollutants in vapor or liquid form
ce:section.ce:para: Some aromatic hydrocarbons like benzene, xylene, ethylbenzene, and toluene are very dangerous pollutants present in the environment. The primary sources of these pollutants are incomplete combustion of automobile exhaust and fuel [75]. The conventional methods such as flame ionisation detectors (FID), mass spectrometers, gas chromatography, etc. have various limitations Additionally, these processes are time-consuming as well as complicated. Hence, carbon-based materials are selected as they are sensitive and simplistic. [76]

I.R.C.Stancu et.al. synthesised carbon-based sensors, functionalized with cobalt tetraphenyl porphyrin (CoTPP) or iron tetraphenyl porphyrin (FeTPP), through non-covalent linkages [77]. These sensors gave excellent results in detecting these aromatic hydrocarbons as pollutants. Carbon surfaces functionalized with iron gave better results as compared to carbon materials functionalized with cobalt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensors for pollutants in water bodies
ce:section.ce:para: pH has a great influence on various chemical changes. If pH changes are monitored using sensors, environmental changes and pollutant levels can be determined. In comparison to orthodox sensors, carbon-based sensors are a facile option. These sensors are Chemi-resistive and eliminate the requirements of having a reference electrode, glass membrane, power supply, etc. to fabricate a conventional pH sensor. [52] Takeda and co-workers produced carbon-based sensors using single-walled CNTs that were acid-treated [78]. These devices were reliable, stable, and gave excellent responses in the environment. Polyaniline was used to functionalize these sensors to increase their selectivity for detecting pollutants in water through pH change (Table 6 ) [79].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensors for detection of hazardous metal contaminants
ce:section.ce:para: One of the most prominent reasons for water and soil pollution is heavy transition metal (HTM) ions. Trace amounts of metals like Zn, Co, Cu, and Mn are vital for the signalling process and metabolism in the human body [3] [Fig. 11 ]. Many essential biochemical reactions depend on these metals. However, when these metals start accumulating in larger concentrations, they become hazardous and pose a serious threat to life. Another category of HTM includes mercury, arsenic, and lead. They are extremely poisonous, even in minute concentrations. The WHO announced the permissible limit of some metals such as Cu, Zn, and Fe in the range of 1–10 ppb. To check the presence of these toxicants in the environment, it is important to develop sensing technologies (chemical or electrochemical) to detect and quantify these contaminants.

Due to their advantages like conductivity, large surface area, size, and flexible functionalization, carbon-based materials are the foremost priority for developing these sensors [80] In addition to the above-mentioned edges that a carbon-based sensor provides, they can also act as adsorbing substrates so that metal ions can be adsorbed on their surface. Electrodes encapsulated with carbon-based materials have been excellent detectors of HTM. The mechanism of their detection involves HTM being deposited on the electrode at a specific potential, followed by the removal of these ions from the electrode This process is known as anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV) [81] The recognition ability of these sensors for metal ions can be increased by introducing functional groups, i.e., by functionalization of carbon surfaces. Identification of metal ions like Pb+2 and Cu+2 was demonstrated by Morton et al., using modified carbon-based sensors through cysteine. The sensor hence synthesised could detect the presence of these toxic metal ions at a concentration of 1 ppb to 15 ppb, respectively. Thiacalixarene was attached to these carbon-based sensors by Wang et al. After this functionalization, the sensors had increased sensitivity to Pb+2 ions. Single-walled CNTs were employed by Kim et al. to detect Hg+2. A chemiresistive sensor was used by Star et al. along with an optical measuring spectroscopic measuring system to detect the presence of Co+2 in the environment. To enhance their selectivity, these sensors were made to undergo non-covalent functionalization using polyazomethine [82]

The over-accumulation of Fe3+ ions is a huge threat to the environment as well as to humans. It can lead to chronic diseases, hypothyroidism, gonad dysfunction, and many diverse diseases. Both excess and deficiency of ferric ions cause types of cancer and anaemia in the body. Therefore, the detection of Fe3+ is necessary. [90,91]

For the sensing of Fe3+ ions, Raji Atchudan and the team devised a synthesis technique for hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots (HN-CDs) [17] The final goldish brown product was noted to be tuneable and strong fluorescence (FL) was emitted by the thus synthesised novel HN-CDs. The Fe3+ ions might form a complex with surface functionalities (−COOH, −OH, and –C–NH) of HN-CHs (Fig. 12 A]). This complex might efficiently avoid the hydrolysis of Fe3+ in the solution, leading to the (FL) quenching of HN-CDs. So, this material is suitable as a nanoprobe for the Fe3+ ion detection in an aqueous solution by the fluorometric method. The HN-CDs material sensitively and selectively detects Fe3+ ions in the range of 1–100 μM, with a detection limit of 0.32μM [90]

Raja Dinesh Kumar and colleagues synthesised fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via Piper betel precursor for fluorometric sensors to detect (50–150nM) Fe3+ in an aqueous medium in the presence of other metal ions such as (Mg2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Fe2+) at the same concentration [Fig. 12.B].

Radioactive uranium compounds as pollutants can damage the biological system of the human body and can cause cancer. According to WHO, the maximum concentration of uranyl ions must not exceed 30 μg/L in drinking water. Mohamed E. Mahmoud’s team synthesised CQDs using the fast, green microwave irradiation pyrolysis methodology of the starch-water system. Poly (anthranilic acid-formaldehyde-phthalic acid) (PAFP) was created by condensation polymerization of 0.1 mol anthranilic acid, 0.2 mol formaldehyde, and 0.1 mol catalyst. Thus, produced PAFP is functionalized with several -COOH to provide sites for covalent binding with-OH groups on CQDs to form an ester linkage. The covalent CQDs bind to a polymeric solid support (PAFP) for the formation of a novel CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent which prevents water solubility of CQDs. The low-cost and simple adsorption technique is considered proficient for removing 95.5–98.0 % Uranium (VI) ions for 30 − 90 μg/L. [Fig. 12.C].[17,21,22]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensors for detection of pollutants in soil
ce:section.ce:para: Diverse carbon-based sensors are being developed for detecting soil pollutants so that crop loss, plant diseases, and poisoning of crops can be reduced. Sensing technology, along with the recruitment of biological elements, represents a sustainable alternative to traditional methods of detecting pollutants in soil. Cheng and co-workers [94] developed a hierarchical macro-microporous composite structure that was used as a lipase-based electrode. These electrodes assisted in detecting a pollution-causing pesticide—nitrogenous diphenyl ether—in the soil. Babolghani et al. performed a study to report the detection of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) using carbon-based sensors functionalized with Cu2O Toxic pollutants (benzene, toluene, etc.) were also detected using these sensors [95] (Table 7 ).

Research and studies have shown that natural and green sources can be successfully employed for synthesising green carbon materials, aimed at developing low-cost, environmentally benign, and resource-saving processes for large-scale production. However, continuous discussions and research need to be undertaken to ensure sustainability at almost all steps. So, it is important to take into consideration that the collection of natural sources, their processing, and hence the fabrication of sensors should take place on-site to avoid additional costs.

The presence of metallic minerals is an important prerequisite for the development of green carbon-based materials. However, this also poses a disadvantage because the presence of such metals or their minerals in the natural sources is either very small or present in small amounts. This causes the uncontrolled or unreproducible development of carbon materials. Hence, the process of generating such materials requires pre-treatments to reduce the probability of such challenges.

Presently, researchers are also facing the major challenge of producing such materials in bulk while retaining their well-defined characteristics. It is a typical task, even if one uses tailored organic precursors. The process becomes even more complex by using natural sources or catalysts.

Green carbon-based sensors have a huge role in environmental remediation given that they have an elaborate structure and possess a large specific surface area for absorbing contaminants. The onset of using green technologies to fabricate sensors that detect toxic pollutants in the environment is a novel and sustainable approach. Though it is a challenging task, nanotechnology, along with green chemistry, accomplishes the goal of environmental sustainability. Manufacturing, designing, and functionalizing these carbon-based materials decreases the generation of waste products and is an eco-friendly process. The use of such carbon-based sensors for detecting potential toxicants and hazardous substrates such as heavy metals, pesticides, harmful gases, etc. has proven to be highly efficient and has a variety of desirable characteristics like increased sensitivity, size, economics, and facile portability. Apart from this, their fabrication method, which is based on the principles of green chemistry, reduces the harmful impacts they might have on our environment. They have the potential to become an excellent and exceptional instrument for development in the field of environmental research.

However, there is an appalling urgency to create a strong platform for the reliable detection and quantification of environmental pollutants. To address this challenge, an approach should be devised that is multidisciplinary and involves the opinions and advice of concerned government authorities, analytical scientists, chemists, biochemists, researchers from the fields of environmental studies, ecology, and biology, experts in risk management, and toxicologists. In addition, research groups or companies that are concerned with the innovation of green carbon-based sensing processes must prepare policies for the safe management of these sensors and the factors that affect them, in order to ensure the safety of the public as well. Despite the advancements in this field, applications on a large and commercial scale remain a challenge. This is because of a lack of control in the synthetic methods of these materials. To overcome this, it can be predicted that functionalizing the surface of carbon-based sensors with various functional groups could be a possible solution for the commercialization and wide applications of these sensors.

Constant research and study in this area are important to boost the use of these sensors on an industrial level. Introducing novel carbon-based materials with unique features such as capacity, selectivity, and capability to undergo functionalization makes them extremely important for functioning as pollutant sensors.
Data will be made available on request.
